title: LLM Basics
lesson:
An LLM stands for Large Language Model. Let's unpack those three words, because each one matters. Language: it works with text. Model: it is a piece of software that has learned patterns from examples, the way a weather model learns patterns from past storms. Large: it is huge, both in how much text it read while learning and in how many internal dials it can adjust. The most popular LLMs in 2026 (the ones behind ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, and open models like Llama and Qwen) read a big slice of the public internet, plus books and code, during training.

Here is the single most useful mental shift for a beginner: an LLM does not look up answers, and it does not think like a person. It predicts the next chunk of text. That's it. Given the words so far, it guesses what word probably comes next, adds it, then guesses again, over and over. Everything impressive an LLM does, from writing an email to explaining a tax form, is built on this one trick repeated thousands of times.

The autocomplete analogy (and why it's only half the story)

Think of the text-suggestion bar on your phone keyboard. You type "I'm running" and it offers "late". That is tiny next-word prediction. An LLM is the same idea scaled up by a factor of millions, and trained so carefully that the "autocomplete" can hold a whole conversation, follow instructions, and write working code. The phone keyboard learned from your messages; an LLM learned from a library the size of a small country. Same engine, wildly different scale.

A second analogy: imagine a chef who has tasted millions of dishes but owns no recipe book. Ask for "a spicy tomato pasta" and they don't fetch a recipe; they produce a plausible dish from a deep gut sense of what usually goes together. That is why LLMs are fluent and creative, and also why they sometimes confidently make things up. A made-up but confident answer is called a hallucination (the model produces fluent text that simply isn't true). The chef never lies on purpose; they just cook what feels right, even when they're wrong.

How a single word actually comes out

Internally the model doesn't see letters. Text is first chopped into small pieces called Tokens, roughly word-fragments, and the model predicts one token at a time. For each step it produces a score for every possible next token. Those raw scores are called logits (the unsquashed numbers the model assigns each candidate token). The scores are turned into probabilities, and one is picked.

Prompt:  "The capital of France is"
Model scores next tokens:
  " Paris"  -> 0.91
  " Lyon"   -> 0.04
  " a"      -> 0.02   (start of "a city...")
Pick " Paris", add it, then repeat.

How adventurous the pick is gets controlled by a setting called temperature (a dial from cautious to creative). Low temperature almost always grabs the top choice, giving safe, repetitive text; high temperature lets unlikely tokens through, giving variety but more risk of nonsense.

Where the "smarts" actually live

The learned knowledge is stored in millions or billions of numbers called Parameters (the adjustable dials, also called weights, set during training). A small model might have a few billion; a frontier model has hundreds of billions. More parameters usually means more capability but also more memory and cost. The number-crunching engine inside nearly every modern LLM is the Transformers architecture, and its key gear is the Attention Mechanism, which lets the model decide which earlier words matter most when predicting the next one.

One more must-know limit: the model can only "see" a fixed amount of text at once. That working memory is the Context Windows. Go past it and the earliest text falls off the edge, like a whiteboard you keep writing on until you must erase the top to fit more.

Calling a hosted model is refreshingly simple. You send text, you get text back:

from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()
resp = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Explain an LLM in one sentence."}],
    temperature=0.3,
)
print(resp.choices[0].message.content)

💡 You don't need to train anything to start. Most real products are built by prompting an existing model well, not by building one. Learn to use them first; learn to Fine-Tuning Basics later, only when prompting truly isn't enough.
mentalModel: An LLM is a giant phone-autocomplete: it never looks up an answer, it just predicts the next text chunk again and again. Fluent because it read a library; sometimes wrong because it's guessing, not knowing.
mistakes: Thinking the LLM "knows" facts like a database. It stores patterns, not records. It can state a fact correctly and then invent a fake one in the same breath, with equal confidence. Always verify anything important.

Believing it reasons or browses by default. A plain LLM only predicts text from what it already saw in training plus your prompt. It can't check today's news or do real math unless you connect it to tools (see Tool Calling).
Assuming bigger always means better. A huge model can be slower, pricier, and overkill. A well-chosen small model often wins on cost and speed (see SLMs (Small Language Models)).
Confusing the model with the chat app. ChatGPT is a product; the LLM is the engine inside it. Memory, file upload, and web search are features bolted around the model, not powers of the model itself.
Forgetting the context window is finite. Paste a 500-page PDF and the earliest pages may silently fall off the edge, so the model answers as if it never saw them.
Trusting confident tone as a truth signal. LLMs sound equally sure when right and when hallucinating. Fluency is not evidence.
exercise:
Open any chat LLM you have access to. Run this tiny experiment to feel next-token prediction at work.

  1. Ask: Finish this line: "The early bird catches the". Note how instantly and confidently it completes a familiar pattern.
  2. Now ask it something obscure and checkable, like What was the exact attendance at the 1953 Llanberis village fete?. Watch whether it admits it doesn't know or invents a precise-sounding number (a hallucination).
  3. Ask the same factual question twice in fresh chats. Do the answers match? Inconsistency is a red flag that it's guessing, not recalling.

Stretch goal: If your tool exposes a temperature setting (via its API or playground), ask the identical creative prompt at temperature 0 and at temperature 1. Describe in one sentence how the variety and reliability change.
solution:
What you should see: Step 1 returns "worm" almost every time, because that completion is overwhelmingly common in the training text. Step 2 often produces a fake but specific number, since no real record exists, so the model fills the gap with plausible-sounding text. Step 3 usually gives different answers across chats, which proves it is generating, not looking up.

Stretch hint: At temperature 0 the answers are nearly identical and safe each run (it keeps grabbing the top token). At temperature 1 you get more varied, surprising, occasionally weirder output. Same engine, different boldness dial.
summary: An LLM is software that predicts the next text token over and over, having learned patterns from a vast pile of text. It is fluent but guessing, which makes hallucinations possible. Its knowledge lives in billions of parameters, it runs on the transformer architecture, and it can only see a limited context window at a time. Start by prompting existing models; reach for fine-tuning only when you must.

keyTerms.def: Software that learned text patterns from huge amounts of writing and generates new text by predicting one chunk at a time.
The core trick: given the text so far, guess the most likely next token, add it, and repeat. Every LLM ability is built on this.
A small piece of text (often a word fragment) that the model reads and predicts. Models work in tokens, not letters or whole words.
One of the millions or billions of adjustable numbers inside the model where its learned knowledge is stored.
The raw, unsquashed scores the model gives every possible next token before they are turned into probabilities.
A setting from cautious to creative. Low picks the safest next token; high allows more surprising, varied output.
When the model produces fluent, confident text that is simply false, because it is generating plausible patterns rather than recalling facts.
The fixed amount of text (prompt plus response) the model can hold in working memory at once. Text beyond it is ignored.
